petitioner_background: The petitioner is a physician and scientist in the field of pulmonary and critical care medicine seeking classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences. Counsel references the petitioner's work at prominent medical institutions, research and clinical work, memberships in medical societies, presentations at conferences, and media attention. The record contains inconsistent pronouns (counsel alternately refers to the petitioner as he/him and she/her).
outcome_reasoning: The appeal was summarily dismissed under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v) because counsel failed to identify specifically any erroneous conclusion of law or statement of fact in the director's decision. Counsel did not challenge any of the director's specific findings or analyses of the documentary evidence submitted under the categories at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3), nor explain how the specific evidence supported eligibility for the criteria at 8 C.F.R. §§ 204.5(h)(3)(ii)-(iv), (v), and (viii). Instead, the appellate submission offered only a general statement asserting the petitioner met at least three criteria without specifying where the director's alleged error occurred. The petitioner also did not provide any additional evidence pertaining to eligibility for the classification sought.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issue: the appeal was summarily dismissed under 8 C.F.R. § 103.3(a)(1)(v) because counsel failed to identify specifically any erroneous conclusion of law or statement of fact in the director's decision, offering only a general assertion that the petitioner met at least three criteria. Counsel's brief also contained inconsistent pronouns, alternately referring to the petitioner as male and female.
